steps.title: Identify what must be shown
steps.content: The researchers explain that rearranging a store's layout causes shoppers to search for relocated items, move through more of the store, encounter more products, and make an unplanned purchase. A supporting survey response must show both a relocated item the shopper had to search for and an unplanned purchase that resulted.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — "I needed to buy some cleaning supplies, but they weren't in their regular place. While I was looking for them, I saw this interesting notebook and decided to buy it, too.": The shopper searched for relocated items (cleaning supplies moved from their regular spot), wandered the store while looking, and made an unplanned purchase (the notebook). Both required elements are present. ✓
  • Choice B — "I didn't buy everything on my shopping list today. I couldn't find a couple of the items in the store, even though I looked all over for them.": The shopper searched but found nothing and made no unplanned purchase. The key outcome — buying something extra — is absent. ✗
  • Choice C — "The store sent me a coupon for a new brand of soup, so I came here to find out what kinds of soup that brand offers. I decided to buy a few cans because I had the coupon.": The purchase was prompted by an external marketing cue (a coupon), exactly the "obvious cue" type of spontaneous purchase the study contrasts with layout rearrangement. ✗
  • Choice D — "This store is larger than one that's closer to where I live, and it carries more products. I came here to buy some things that the other store doesn't always have.": The shopper chose this store intentionally for its size and selection. No layout rearrangement, no relocated items, no unplanned purchase. ✗
That matches Choice A — "I needed to buy some cleaning supplies, but they weren't in their regular place. While I was looking for them, I saw this interesting notebook and decided to buy it, too.".
